title: The teenager with stroke-like episodes that cross no artery
blurb: Maternally inherited episodes of cortical dysfunction with lactic acidosis — a respiratory-chain disorder where tissues full of oxygen still can't make ATP.

patient.complaint: Sudden vision loss and confusion, recurrent migraines, short stature.

opening: 15-year-old boy, short for his family, with recurrent severe migraines and now an acute episode of right-sided vision loss, confusion, and a focal seizure. MRI shows a parieto-occipital lesion that does NOT respect a vascular territory. Serum and CSF lactate are elevated. He is hearing-impaired and has had myoclonus. His mother and maternal uncle have diabetes and deafness. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Maternal-line history: mother and maternal relatives with diabetes, deafness, and 'migraines' (maternal/mitochondrial inheritance). Short stature, exercise intolerance, sensorineural hearing loss. Several prior stroke-like episodes with partial recovery.
exam: Short stature, sensorineural hearing loss. Acutely: homonymous visual field deficit, confusion, post-ictal. Mild proximal myopathy. No carotid bruit. Fundi with pigmentary changes.

prompts.cause: What part of the electron transport chain is most impaired, and what's the inheritance?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why are brain and muscle hit hardest, and why the lactic acidosis despite normal oxygenation?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the first ETC complex whose function fails when mitochondrial protein synthesis is impaired here.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — Complex I. The m.3243A>G tRNA mutation cripples mitochondrial translation, hitting the (mtDNA-encoded) respiratory complexes; Complex I (the largest, most mtDNA-dependent) bears the brunt. NADH can't be reoxidized → high-energy-demand tissues (brain, muscle) fail and pyruvate is shunted to lactate.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Pick the entry complex that oxidizes NADH — the most mtDNA-dependent and the most affected.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Complex I (NADH → NAD⁺). MELAS impairs mitochondrial translation, reducing Complex I (and others); the ETC stalls and lactate climbs despite full tissue oxygenation.

mcqs.stem: Why does MELAS follow maternal inheritance with variable severity among relatives?
choices: mtDNA is maternally transmitted, and heteroplasmy (the proportion of mutant mtDNA per tissue) sets a threshold for disease expression
It is an autosomal recessive nuclear gene
mcqs.explanation: Mitochondria (and their DNA) come from the egg → maternal inheritance. Each cell carries a mix of normal and mutant mtDNA (heteroplasmy); symptoms appear once the mutant fraction passes a tissue-specific threshold, explaining the wide variability among maternal relatives.

mcqs.stem: Why is lactate elevated despite normal arterial oxygenation?
choices: A failing ETC can't reoxidize NADH, so pyruvate is reduced to lactate to regenerate NAD⁺ for glycolysis
mcqs.explanation: Oxygen delivery is fine, but the respiratory chain can't USE it efficiently. NADH accumulates, NAD⁺ becomes limiting, and the cell ferments pyruvate to lactate (via LDH) to keep glycolysis running — a recurring theme across ETC disorders and cyanide poisoning.

mcqs.stem: What does the muscle biopsy classically show?
choices: Ragged-red fibers — subsarcolemmal accumulation of abnormal mitochondria on Gomori trichrome
mcqs.explanation: Compensatory mitochondrial proliferation produces ragged-red fibers; many are COX (Complex IV)-deficient. SDH (Complex II, nuclear-encoded) staining is retained — a clue that the defect is in mtDNA-encoded subunits.
